t . rr
Lal'LEOrr:.!.a
Fair P01itical
Practices Commission
May 10, 1990
Barbara MiLman
staff counsel
Assembly Rules committee
senatcr Hotel
l72l L Street, Roon 400
Sacramento, cA 9 5814
Re:
Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-9 0-2 0 6
Dear llts. I.Iilnan:
This is in response to your letter requesting advice concern-
ing the campaign provj.sions of the Po1itical Reform Act (the
ttaslrr1.1/ Since your questions are general in nature and you have
not naned a specific elected official 
on whose behalf you have
requested this advice, we can,only provide the following informal
responses to your questions. ' /
OUESTION
All of the folloving nailers which pronote a candidate's
election to office and also include references to other candidates
vitl be paid for by the candidate's canpaign funds and will be
prepared, paid for, and circulated without the j.nvolvement of
other candidates. Are expenditures by the candidate for the nail-
ers perrnitted under the rrtrust. and rrtransferrr provj-sions of the
Act?
a. A slate mailer tistj.ng the candidate and other
candidates .
b. A canpaign rnailer which
candidate with another candidate. includes a photograph of the
L/ Goverruuent Code Sections 81CJO-91015. Al,L statutory refer-
ences are to the Government Code lrnless otherwise indicated. 
com-
rnission reg'ulations appear at 2 california code of Reg\rtations
Section 180oo, et seq. A11 references to regulations are to Tj.t1e
2, Division 5 of the California Code of Regulations.
2/ rnfornal assistance does not
inmunity provided by an opinion
83114; ReguLation 18329(c) (3) . )
provide the requestor with the
or fornal written advice. (Section
428 J Street, Suite 8OO . P.O. Box 8O? o Sacramento CA 95804.0807 . (916)322.5660


File No. I-90-206
?age 2
c. A canpaign mailer vhich includes an endorsement of the
candidate by another candidate.
d. A campaign nailer which includes the photograph or refer-
ences to the candidate's opponent.
CONCLUSION
canpaign funds roay be used to pay for a slate mailer which
endorses other candidates, or canpa.i.gn nailers which include a
photograph of the candidate wj.th another candidate, an endorsement
of the candidate by another eandidate, or photographs or refer-
ences to the candidate's opponent, provided the nailings are
circulated only in the candidate's district 
and the nailers will
not be circulated at the behest of the other candidate or
candidates .
FACTS
Your questions pertain to the following hypothetical situa-
tion:
candidate A is running for nayor, and Candidate B is an
incunbent Assenbl)rnenber who is running for reelection to the As-
senbly. candidate A wishes to circulate a variety of mailings,
including a slate rnailer, a nailing listing candidates that
endorse hin, nailings including photos of hin rrith various
statewide elected officials, 
and mailers that critigue his
opponent's record. Al-1 of these mailers will contaj.n the nane,
photograph or reference to the AsseEbly candidate. A11 the mail-
ers will be paid for with Candidate A's canpaign funds. Final1y,
all the nailers will be prepared, paid for and circulated
independently of Candidate B, the Assembly candidate.
ANAI,YSI S
The Transfer Prohibition
In June of 1988, Proposition 73 enacted new restrictions on
the use of carnpaign funds that have been incorporated into the
Act. (Section 85202(b); Section 85304.) Section 85304 provides:
No candidate for elective office or cornmittee
controLled by that candidate or candidates for
elective office shall transfer any contribution to
any other candidate for elective office. 
Transfers
of funds between candidates or their cotrtrolled
conmittees are prohibited.
This section has been interpreted to prohibit the transfer of
campaign funds or canpaign assets betlreen candidates. In our
telephone conversation f mistakenlir rs1..=.d to the statutory
provision at issue in your questions as the transfer provision,
Section 85304. fn fact, under all the hypotheticals you
presented, the transfer provision is not implicated.


File No. I-90-206
Prge l
fn each hypothetical, the candidate circulating the rnailing
is paying for the Eailinq from his own canpaign funds. Further
the roailing is produced, paid for and circulated lrithout anv
involvement of the other candidate. Since the Assenbly candidate
is not involved in the nailing, he presumably does not provide
goods, services or money to the nayoral candidate, and thus, the
Assenblynenber does not violate the transfer provision. 
The
provision of an endorsement to the nayoral candidate alone is
merely the provision of volunteer personal services which is
expressly exempted fron the definition of rrcontributionrr in the
Act. (Section 82015; In re Adams (1976) 2 FPPC Ops. ]-27, copy
enclosed. )
Uoreover, since all the nailings will be prepared, paid for
and circulated independently of the AsseEbl)menber and not at the
Assemblynenber's behest,r/ paynent for the nai.Ler by the nayoral
candidate woul,d not be treated as a contribution to the Assenbly
candidate. Thus, the uayoral candidate would also not be in
violation of the transfer provision.
Ho!,rever, if the nailing is produced at the behest of the As-
senbly candidate, the naiLer will be treated as an in-kind
contribution to the Assenbly candidate and lrould violate the
transfer provision of the Act. Under such circumstances the cost
of the rnailing would have to be divided between candidates
participating in the circulation of the uailing and each would
have to pay their pro-rata share directly to the vendor to ensure
that the transfer provision has not been violated. 
(olson Advice
Letter, A-89-597, copy enclosed. )
The Trust Provision
Section 85201(c) requires candidates for public office to
deposit all canpaign contributions they receive into a campaign
bank account.
section 85202 (b) states:
A11 contributions deposited into the campaign
account shall be deemed to be held in trust for
expenses associated with the election of the
candidate to the specific office for lrhich the
candidate has stated, pursuant to Section 852OO,
that he or she intends to seek or expenses associ-
ated uith holding that office.
3/ 
rMade at the behest" is defined in Reg"ulation 1g215 (copy
enclosed) to include any pa)rnent nade under the control or lt ttre
direction of, in cooperation, consultation, coordination, or
concert with, or at the request or suggestion of a candidate,
controlled conmittee, official 
conmittee of a political party, or
organization formed or existing prinarily for political 
purposes.


Fl■e No. ■-90-206
Page l
Section 85801 states in pertinent part:
An expenditure to seek office is within the
Iawful execution of the trust iruposed by Section
85202 if it is reasonably related to a political
purpose. An expenditure associated with holding
office is withi.n the lawful execution of the trust
inposed by Section 85202 Lf it is reasonably
related to a legislative or governnental purpose.
Expenditures which confer a substantial personal
beneflt sha11 be directly related to a political,
legislative, or governnental purpose.
Thus, the question you present turns upon whether the
candidate's campaign funds are properly spent under Sections
8s202 (b) and 85801.
The expenditure of canpaign funds for the purposes set forth
in your letter is obviously not an expenditure that has a
legislative or governmental purpose. Therefore, such expenditures
do not satisfy that part of Section 85801 which pertnits
expenditures associated with holding office.
More problernatic is vhether the expenditures are for seekingr
office. 
section 85801 appears to say that anv expendi.ture of
canpai,gn funds which is "reasonably related to a political
purposerr is a legitinate expenditure for seeking office. 
However,
an exanination of other provj-sions of the Act and of the history
of Section 85202(b) indicate that this is not the case.
First, Sections 85802, 85802.5, 85804 and 85805 clearly linit
the broad language of Section 85801. Second, Section 85202(b)
itself must be given neaning beyond that of Section 85801.
At the tiure of Proposition 73,s passage, the only restric-
tions upon a candidate,s use of caupaign funds were set forth in
Elections Code Sections 12400 through 12407. These restrictions
prohibited a candidate,s personal use of canpaign funds (Elections
code Sections 12401 through 12403) and set forth requirements for
the disposal of surplus campaign funds (Electj.ons Code Section
]-2404r.
The passage of Proposition 73 inposed additional restrictj-ons
on the use of canpaign funds. In addition to Section 85304, Sec-
tion 85202 (b) explicitly states that such funds are held in trust
and can only be spent for seeking electj,ve office or for expenses
associated with holding office. 
Since the personal use restric-
tions of the Elections Code were already in effect when pr^posi-
tion 73 passed, it seeued clear that Seation 85202(b) hacl a mean-
ing beyond those provisions. Indeed, established ruies of
statutory construction required an interpretation in which each
word or phrase of Proposition 73 lras presumed to have rnean j,ng.
(Rooers v. Alvas (1984) 160 Cal.App.3d 997, 1004.)


Fi■e
paoe
I-90-206
Ef f ec'-1ve January 1, 1990, the Legj-slature revised the
personal use provisions of the Elections code and moved them to
chapter 5 of the Act (see Stats. 1989, ch. 1452.) As part of this
revj,sj.on, the Legislature added Section 85801, as rre11 as Sectj-ons
85800 and 85802 through 85807.
Thj-s new personal use law undoubtedly contains much more
expLicit ;equirements than the previous version of that law.
However. it does not essentiall-y alter the rules that previously
applied to cietermj,ne whether or not campaign funds were
legitinatel"y expended for seeking eLective office. 
The ner., ver-
sj,on of ihe 1aw, therefore, does not aLter the j.nperative that
Secrj.on 85202 (b) be given a neaning beyond that of traditional,
personal use. Furthemore, section 85801, as literaLly applied,
raises a serious question as to rrhether that section constitutes
an amendmen-- r-hat furthers the purposes of +-he Act. 
(See Section
IL012 (a) . )
Accordingly, the conmission, both before and after the effec-
tive date of the new personal use 1aw, has interpreted Sections
85202 (b) and 85801 to require a direct relationship betlreen a
candidate's canpaign expenditure and his or her campaign for of-
fice in order for the expenditure to be legitinately associated
with seeking that office. 
In addition, since the effective date
of the new personal use Iaw, such an expenditure is not pernitted
if it otherwise violates the prohibitions set forth in Sections
85802, 85802.5, 85804 or 85805. For example, if a candidate
expends canpaign funds for nonspecialty clothing, the expenditure
arguably has a reasonable relationship to a political 
purpose
under Section 85801 in that the candidate needs to appear well-
dressed in uaking carupaign appearances. However, Section 85802(d)
specifically prohibits such a purchase with canpaign funds.
Likewise, where a candidate transfers caurpaign funds to a
political action conmittee for its use in naking contributions to
or independent expenditures on other candidates, the transfer has
no direct relationship to the candidate's own campaign for office
and is not pernitted under Section 85202(b). (see Dowell Advice
Letter, No. A-89-566, copy enclosed.)
Your questions nust be exanined in light of this history. 
In
your hypothetical guestions, the mayoral candidate intends to nake
expenditures frou his canpaign funds for the nailers to pronote
his own el,ectj-on to office. 
The purpose for inclusion of the
other candidates j-n the nailer would be to lend greater cred-
ibility 
to the mailer and the nayoral candidate. Thus, the
predoninant purpose behind the uailing is to further the
candidate,s own canpaign. ConsequentLy, we conclude .:hat
expenditures for such nailers in the candidate,s orrn district
vhich are intended to further the candidate,s election to off i.ce
have a direct relation to the nayoral candidaters canpaign for
office. 
The fact that the nailers have an incidental benefit to
other candidates does not Dake the expenditure an independent
expenditure in violation of Section 85202 (b) .
０
甘
劇
　
一
つ


9i1e No. l-90-206
?a -cle a
Please note, however, that if the mailer is cir:ulated
outside the jurisdiction in rrhich the mayoral candi.date is running
for efection, the requisite relationship to seeking office would
be roissing. (Propper Advice Letter, No. I-86-258, copy enclosed.)
Ilor exanple, r,rhere a nailer is sent into the AsseEblymenber's
district 
and not into the uayoral candidate's distrj.ct, the nailer
bears no direct relationship to the Dayoral candidate/s elect.ion.
Under such cj.:cumscances the trust provision would be violated.
If you have any further questions regarding ihis aatter,
please feel free to contact De at (916) 322-59oL.
S incere Iy,
Kathryn E. Donovan
ceneraL Counsel
